critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment is one of the more carefully designed cases in this collection, with correct temporal awareness in the preprocessing section ('All transformations are fit on the training set only'), appropriate metric selection, and a reasonable baseline comparison. However, there is a critical split strategy flaw: Section 2 explicitly describes using 'stratified random sampling across all available months,' selecting random 15% and 15% for validation and test. This means patients admitted in 2022 can appear in both training and test sets. Despite the design's stated 'recognition that patient data contains inherent temporal dependencies,' the actual split implementation is random — directly contradicting this stated intent.

Second, the RNN and logistic regression are compared on different feature types. The RNN receives raw time-series vital signs (168-hour sequences of 4-hour measurements), while the logistic regression receives aggregated statistics derived from these same sequences (mean, std, slope). The design frames this as the 'fair' comparison isolating temporal modeling — but these are not equivalent inputs. The LR baseline has access to summary statistics that may capture the majority of the temporal signal (e.g., trend slope, variance), making the 'temporal structure' hypothesis test weaker than claimed.

Third, the bidirectional LSTM processes 'sequences of up to 168 hours / 7 days of hospitalization' but patients with shorter stays will have truncated sequences. Padding strategy and masking are not specified — zero-padding can introduce spurious temporal features that bias LSTM predictions.

The evaluation section is otherwise well-specified, with AUC-ROC as the primary metric, precision-recall AUC as a secondary, and calibration plots included. These are appropriate for a clinical readmission setting.
all_issues_raised: Critical split strategy contradiction: despite acknowledging temporal dependencies, the implementation uses stratified random sampling — patients from the same calendar period appear in both training and test sets

Feature set asymmetry: RNN receives raw time-series sequences while LR receives aggregated statistics (mean, std, trend slope) — the comparison partly tests aggregation loss rather than temporal modeling capacity

Sequence padding strategy not specified: variable-length hospitalization sequences require zero-padding, but masking behavior is not described — spurious LSTM learning on padding tokens is a known confound
Early stopping criterion is validation F1-score for RNN but model selection uses AUC-ROC — the training and selection objectives are inconsistent
No patient-level stratification: multiple admissions from the same patient in train and test sets inflate apparent generalization via patient-specific learned patterns

Stratified random split ignores temporal dependencies despite acknowledging them, creating temporal leakage for a longitudinal prediction task — a patient from month 30 can appear in training while a month 10 patient is in test
The LSTM and logistic regression receive different feature representations (raw sequences vs. aggregates), conflating architecture with feature type and testing a narrower hypothesis than claimed
No statistical significance test or confidence interval methodology is specified for the primary AUC-ROC comparison, leaving the main claim without inferential support
Planned readmission exclusion criterion is unoperationalized — no mechanism is described for distinguishing planned from unplanned readmissions, risking label contamination

Bidirectional LSTM is used for intra-admission sequences, but bidirectionality requires looking at future time steps within the admission to predict the same encounter's outcome; this is inappropriate if any features in the 'future' part of the admission sequence occur after clinical decisions that determine discharge disposition

Random admission-level split for longitudinal EHR data with multiple admissions per patient creates patient-level leakage — same patient's records appear across train, validation, and test sets, violating independence
Random split does not respect temporal ordering — model may train on a patient's 2023 admission and test on their 2021 admission, inverting causal ordering and creating temporal leakage

Bidirectional LSTM processes full hospitalization sequences including discharge-day vitals, which may serve as proxies for discharge disposition — a near-future feature that is clinically proximal to the readmission outcome
Multiple admissions per patient treated as independent observations inflates effective sample size and violates the independence assumption for statistical tests and performance estimation

The design claims balanced temporal representation across splits (all time periods in all sets) while also claiming temporal analysis — these goals are contradictory and indicate the split cannot support causal temporal inference
